# Triggery

> Declarative, hook-first business-logic orchestration layer for React, Solid, and Vue. Triggery is not an event emitter and not a state manager — it sits between events, your store of choice, and side effects, and declares scenarios of the form `event → conditions → actions` in one file. State lives in Zustand / Redux / Jotai / MobX / signals. Events come from anywhere (WebSockets, DOM, your UI). Triggery decides when to do what, if the world is in the right shape.

Triggery is pre-1.0. The API is unstable; minor versions may introduce breaking changes. Source of truth for everything below: <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery>.

## What Triggery is for

- Coordinate side effects and cross-feature scenarios that currently live in `useEffect`, sagas, observable middleware, listener middleware, or thunks.
- Express "when X happens, do Y if Z is true" as a first-class object with inspection, devtools, tests, and tooling.
- Keep UI components dumb: they only fire events and provide data/executors through typed hooks.

## What Triggery is *not*

- Not a state manager. State stays in Zustand / Redux / Jotai / MobX / signals.
- Not a reactive engine. There is no incremental graph and no propagation pipeline; conditions are read at fire time.
- Not a finite-state machine library. Use XState if you need formal state charts.
- Not an event emitter. Events are typed and routed via an indexed dispatch, not pattern-matched at runtime.

## Architecture in 6 bullets

- `createTrigger<Schema>(config)` declares a scenario in one file: events, conditions, required gate, handler.
- `createRuntime()` instantiates an isolated runtime: indexed dispatch (`Map<eventKey, RuntimeTrigger[]>`), microtask scheduler by default, middleware chain, inspector ring buffer.
- Conditions are pull-only: `useCondition` stores a getter, the runtime calls it at fire time. No re-render storms.
- Actions are last-mount-wins by default with `useAction`, with chainable `actions.debounce(ms).x(...)` / `actions.throttle(ms).x(...)` and async concurrency strategies (`take-latest` default).
- Async handlers receive an `AbortSignal`. `take-latest` aborts the previous run. HMR aborts in-flight runs with reason `hmr`.
- Inspector keeps a ring buffer of recent runs (`useInspectHistory`, `<InspectorView>`). Devtools middleware streams to Redux DevTools via `@triggery/devtools-redux`.

## Documentation

- [README](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/README.md): pitch, install, the canonical scenario example, packages, performance.
- [CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md): development setup, repo layout, workflow, coding standards, tests, benchmarks, changesets, commits, PRs, RFCs.
- [ROADMAP](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/ROADMAP.md): phases to 1.0 and V2.
- [GOVERNANCE](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/GOVERNANCE.md): decision-making, maintainer responsibilities, RFC process.
- [MAINTAINERS](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/MAINTAINERS.md): current maintainers and contact info.
- [SECURITY](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/SECURITY.md): private vulnerability reporting via GitHub Security Advisories.
- [SUPPORT](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/SUPPORT.md): where to ask questions, file bugs, propose features.
- [CHANGELOG](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md): per-package changelogs are generated by changesets.
- [Benchmarks](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/benchmarks/COMPARISONS.md): full side-by-side breakdown vs effector / rxjs / saga / xstate / reatom / mobx.

## Packages

### Core

- `@triggery/core` — runtime, `createTrigger`, `createRuntime`, indexed dispatch, inspector, middleware, `graph()`. Source: `packages/core`.
- `@triggery/testing` — `createTestRuntime`, `mockCondition`, `mockAction`, `flushMicrotasks`. Source: `packages/testing`.
- `@triggery/vite` — Vite plugin: auto-imports every `*.trigger.ts` via a virtual module + HMR. Source: `packages/vite`.

### Framework bindings (same API across all three)

- `@triggery/react` — `useEvent`, `useCondition`, `useAction`, `useInlineTrigger`, `useInspectHistory`, `createNamedHooks`, `<TriggerRuntimeProvider>`, `<TriggerScope>`. Source: `packages/react`.
- `@triggery/solid` — same API, native to signals + `onCleanup`. Source: `packages/solid`.
- `@triggery/vue` — same API, `provide`/`inject` + `onScopeDispose`. Source: `packages/vue`.

### State adapters (bridge any store into a Triggery condition)

- `@triggery/zustand` — `useZustandCondition(trigger, name, store, selector)`.
- `@triggery/redux` — `useReduxCondition(trigger, name, store, selector)`.
- `@triggery/jotai` — `useJotaiCondition(trigger, name, store, atom, selector?)`.
- `@triggery/mobx` — `useMobxCondition(trigger, name, () => observable)` without tracking the host.
- `@triggery/reatom` — `useReatomCondition(trigger, name, atom, selector?)` (Reatom v1000+).
- `@triggery/signals` — `useSignalCondition(trigger, name, signal, selector?)` (`@preact/signals-core`, `alien-signals`, any `peek()` / `.value`-shaped signal).
- `@triggery/query` — `useQueryCondition(trigger, name, queryClient, queryKey, selector?)` (TanStack Query cache).

### Event-source adapters

- `@triggery/dom` — `useDomEvent`, `useResizeObserver`, `useIntersectionObserver`.
- `@triggery/socket` — `useSocketIoEvent` (socket.io-client) and `useWebSocketEvent` (native WebSocket).

### DevTools

- `@triggery/devtools-redux` — middleware that streams runtime events into the Redux DevTools browser extension.
- `@triggery/devtools-panel` — drop-in React components for in-app inspection: `<InspectorView>`, `<TriggerSnapshotView>`.
- `@triggery/devtools-bridge` — `installDevtoolsBridge(runtime)` page-side bridge for external inspectors.
- `extensions/chrome-devtools` — Chrome DevTools panel that consumes `@triggery/devtools-bridge`. Load unpacked.

## Canonical example (full source in `README.md` and `examples/vite-react-counter/`)

```ts
// triggers/message.trigger.ts
import { createTrigger } from '@triggery/core';

export const messageTrigger = createTrigger<{
  events: { 'new-message': { author: string; text: string; channelId: string } };
  conditions: {
    user: { id: string; name: string };
    settings: { sound: boolean; notifications: boolean };
    activeChannelId: string | null;
  };
  actions: { showToast: { title: string; body: string }; playSound: 'beep' | 'mod-alert' };
}>({
  id: 'message-received',
  events: ['new-message'],
  required: ['user', 'settings'],
  handler({ event, conditions, actions, check }) {
    if (!conditions.user || !conditions.settings) return;
    if (conditions.activeChannelId === event.payload.channelId) return;
    if (event.payload.author === conditions.user.name) return;
    if (!check.is('settings', (s) => s.notifications)) return;
    actions.showToast?.({ title: event.payload.author, body: event.payload.text });
    if (check.is('settings', (s) => s.sound)) actions.playSound?.('beep');
  },
});
```

UI components plug into named ports:

```tsx
useCondition(messageTrigger, 'user', () => currentUser, [currentUser]);
useCondition(messageTrigger, 'settings', () => prefs, [prefs.sound, prefs.notifications]);
useCondition(messageTrigger, 'activeChannelId', () => currentChannelId, [currentChannelId]);
const fire = useEvent(messageTrigger, 'new-message');
useEffect(() => socket.on('msg', fire), [fire]);
useAction(messageTrigger, 'showToast', ({ title, body }) => toast.success(title, { description: body }));
useAction(messageTrigger, 'playSound', (kind) => audioBus.play(kind));
```

## Performance reference

- Core dispatch hot path is benchmarked on CodSpeed simulation (Valgrind-based cycle counting). Side-by-side comparisons against other libraries are benchmarked in CodSpeed walltime mode for honest apples-to-apples results.
- Dashboard: <https://codspeed.io/triggeryjs/triggery>.
- Methodology, idiomatic implementations per library, and per-scenario analysis: [`benchmarks/COMPARISONS.md`](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/benchmarks/COMPARISONS.md).
